result:
I’m going to take you on a long, looping walk through a single idea that has been rattling around my head for years: the possibility that everything we call “real” is simply a generous, collaborative hallucination that we keep refreshing because we are terrified of being alone with the raw data of existence. The argument is not original—every culture has some version of it—but I want to chase it until my lungs burn, because the chase itself is the point. If you stay with me, we will pass through neuroscience, children’s games, the smell of old bookshops, the death of my first dog, the first time I tasted durian, the last time I lied to someone I loved, and the peculiar way that coral reefs glow under moonlight. I make no promise that we will arrive anywhere in particular; I only promise that I will keep walking for roughly 2,500 words, and if your feet get tired you can rest on a curb while I circle the block again.

One: The Agreement
We start with the basic contract: you agree that the pixels or ink in front of you cohere into symbols, the symbols into words, the words into meaning. I agree that your inner voice is presently reading these marks in whatever accent it uses when no one else is listening. Already we are huddled inside a shared hallucination, because the marks themselves have no voice; they are simply dark squiggles on a lighter ground. Yet here we are, whispering to each other across time zones and possibly centuries. The miracle is not that we manage to communicate; the miracle is that we forget how impossible communication ought to be. Every conversation is a high-wire act performed without a net, convinced the wire is a boulevard.

Two: The Laboratory
In the late 1990s, researchers at Princeton ran an experiment in which a volunteer sat in a chair watching a bank of LEDs. A computer tracked the person’s eye movements and changed the location of the LEDs whenever the eyes performed a saccade—a rapid jerk from one fixation point to another. The volunteer, however, perceived the lights as rock-steady. The conclusion was that the brain fills in the gaps, stitches the world together between blinks, and then sells the doctored footage to consciousness as live breaking news. We literally see a world that isn’t there during the moments when we are, by any physical measure, blind. If the brain is that cavalier about the visual field, what else is it Photoshopping while we aren’t looking? Answer: almost everything. Memories, moods, the sense that you are the same person who woke up this morning, the conviction that your keys are on the hook by the door—none of it comes with a guarantee of veracity. And yet, oddly, the hallucination is predictive, not random; it keeps you alive more often than it gets you killed. That is the only credential reality needs.

Three: The Sandbox
Watch a four-year-old alone with a stick. In under a minute the stick becomes a sword, a magic wand, a snake, a bridge, a measuring device for the height of giants. The child does not announce the transformation; she simply rotates the object in space and the ontology updates. Adults smile and call it imagination, but the child is running the same neural routine the Princeton LEDs triggered. She is training herself to survive inside a species that survives by telling stories faster than entropy can unravel them. The difference is that she has not yet signed the social contract that demands we agree on one consensual layer of pretend and label it “real.” Give her another decade and she will be mortified to remember that she once talked to an action figure. The figure, of course, has not stopped talking; she just stopped listening.

Four: The Bookshop
When I was nineteen, I worked in a second-hand bookshop that smelled of mildew and dust jackets. Every Sunday an elderly woman came in wearing white gloves, a pillbox hat, and a melancholy so fragrant you could taste it. She bought only one thing: a slim volume of Rilke’s “Sonnets to Orpheus” in the original German, which we never had in stock. Each week I apologized; each week she nodded as though I had confirmed an appointment with destiny. One day I asked why she didn’t simply order it online. She smiled the way you smile at a kitten that has offered to do your taxes. “If I found it,” she said, “I would have to admit that I have nothing left to look for.” Then she turned and left, and the bell above the door laughed at both of us. I never saw her again. Years later I realized she was teaching me that the search is the object, the itch is the orgasm, and the void we keep trying to fill is itself a shape we invented so we could long for it.

Five: The Dog
His name was Bishop, a black Labrador who believed with evangelical fervor that every thrown tennis ball was a prayer that would one day return as paradise. When cancer closed his throat, the vet said the humane thing was to let him go while he could still breathe without shame. I carried him on a towel into the clinic, his paws draped over my forearms like wet rope. The last thing he did was lick my wrist where the pulse beats, as if to say, “I taste the story you’re still telling; don’t waste it.” Then the drug hit and the story stopped. I walked out into the parking lot and the world had been demoted from color to greyscale. For weeks I hallucinated him in the periphery—tail flashing around corners, the click of claws on hardwood, the phantom weight of his head on my knee while I read. Eventually the hallucination thinned and evaporated, but not because I “accepted reality.” It evaporated because my brain updated the model: dog = no longer predictive of food in bowl, walks at dawn, fur on sofa. The grief, though—that stayed, because grief is the lag between an outdated map and the territory it once described. In that lag lives every poem ever written.

Six: The Fruit
Durian smells like gasoline poured over custard left to warm in a gym sock. In Singapore they ban it on subways. The first time I tried it, I was backpacking through Penang with a woman I would later follow to Barcelona and lose in Berlin. We sat on plastic stools at 2 a.m. while an uncle split the thorny husk with a machete. The flesh was the color of old ivory, the texture of overcooked banana. I gagged on the fumes; she laughed so hard she snorted. Then something shifted: the sulfur note receded, and behind it bloomed a complicated sweetness—vanilla, almond, a trace of onion that made the sweet brighter by contrast. The shift was not in the fruit but in my brain, which had to reconfigure an entire category of “edible” to keep me from starving in an unfamiliar biome. We finished the whole globe, licking our fingers like thieves. Two years later, when we said goodbye on a platform in Friedrichstraße, she handed me a shrink-wrapped durian segment through the train window. The smell instantly collapsed time: I was back on the curb in Penang, convinced that love could be stretched across continents like taffy. The train pulled away; the segment stayed uneaten in my backpack until it began to leak and I threw it away somewhere outside Hannover. Love, it turns out, has a shorter shelf life than durian.

Seven: The Reef
In 2019 I learned to scuba on the Great Barrier Reef during the third mass-bleaching event. Coral polyps are tiny animals that live in apartments made of their own skeletons; inside those skeletons they farm algae that photosynthesize sugar for rent. When the water warms even a degree above the long-term average, the polyps panic and evict the algae, and the reef turns the color of bone. What scientists didn’t know until recently is that some corals fluoresce just before they die, emitting neon proteins that act as sunscreen. Under moonlight the reef glows electric—turquoise, magenta, ultraviolet—like a last-ditch billboard begging the ocean to notice. Nothing in the evolutionary playbook prepared the coral for humans, so it resorts to beauty as a final argument. I dove through that underwater aurora and felt the familiar hallucination wobble: the conviction that beauty must have a rescuer, that awe obligates the universe to hit undo. Later, on the boat, I vomited salt water and understood that the glow was not an appeal but a resignation letter, written in a language humans can’t read without translating it into guilt.

Eight: The Mirror
Neuroscientists map the social brain by putting people inside fMRI scanners and asking them to play cyberball, a virtual game of catch. If the computer stops throwing the ball to the human, the anterior cingulate cortex lights up—the same area that responds to physical pain. We are wired to feel ostracism as a wound, even when we know it is pixels on a screen. But here is the stranger twist: if you give the person a placebo pill described as “social pain reliever,” the hurt diminishes. The hallucination of relief overrides the halluciation of rejection. Reality, in other words, is a series of nested Russian dolls: each layer is a pretend that only works if you forget you are pretending. The moment you remember, the doll becomes hollow and you glimpse the next doll down, until eventually you run out of dolls and find yourself holding nothing but the idea of a container.

Nine: The Lie
The last time I lied to someone I loved, I said, “I’m fine.” I was not fine; I was hemorrhaging inside, but I wanted to spare her the sight of blood. The lie was altruistic, which is the most dangerous kind, because it dresses up as a sacrament. She believed me, because the alternative was to disbelieve the person she trusted most, and that would have cracked the shared hallucination we called “us.” The crack, of course, appeared anyway: a hairline fracture that widened under the weight of future silences. One afternoon she asked, “Do you ever feel like we’re just standing on opposite sides of a painting, each insisting the sky we see is the real one?” I nodded, because nodding was easier than admitting I no longer knew which side of the canvas I stood on. Weeks later she left. What dies in a breakup is not a person but a joint story; the characters survive, but the plot dissolves, and they wander the margins of each other’s dreams like actors whose show has been cancelled mid-season.

Ten: The Loop
Consciousness is recursive: a story about itself listening to itself talk. The loop folds time into a Möbius strip where beginning and end are the same seam. That is why nostalgia hurts: it is the present trying to haunt the past, like a flashlightbeam aimed into a mirror. The strip only has one side, so walking eventually brings you back to the spot where you started, but flipped—your heart now beats on the opposite side of your chest, and the ache tastes of places you swear you’ve never been. The self is a palimpsest: every time you think you have erased the previous draft, ultraviolet light reveals the older text bleeding through.

Eleven: The Cure
There is no cure for the hallucination, but there are analgesics. One of them is art, which is just a controlled breakdown of consensus. When the dancer throws herself across the stage and the audience gasps, the gasp is the sound of reality wobbling. For a second the shared map is redrawn: gravity becomes negotiable, grief becomes a pirouette, the body remembers it was once stardust rehearsing for shape. The moment passes, but the afterimage lingers like the glow of the coral, and you walk back into the street mildly unsure whether traffic lights mean what you were told they mean.

Twelve: The Street
After my mother’s stroke, the right side of her world simply stopped existing—not gone dark, gone un-invented. She ate only the food on the left half of her plate, shaved only the left side of her face, read only the left half of headlines. The neurologist called it hemispatial neglect, but I called it a radical act of editing. The universe had become a rough draft she refused to revise. One day I placed her wheelchair beside a window that overlooked the park. Children were playing tag, runners passed with dogs, leaves flicked coins of light onto the sidewalk. I asked what she saw. “A blank,” she said, “like a screen after the movie ends.” I moved to stand in her blind field. She startled, as if I had teleported. I waved; she stared through me. I leaned forward until my face filled the missing half of her vision. Slowly, like a Polaroid developing, her eyes focused. “Oh,” she whispered, “you’re there.” For an instant the hallucination re-included me, and the relief felt like love, even though it was only neurons re-recruiting themselves into the shape of a son.

Thirteen: The Count
We are roughly 2500 words in, and I promised to stop when the odometer rolled over. But the joke is that the odometer is itself imaginary, calibrated to a base-ten system invented by primates who counted on their opposable thumbs. If you have stayed this long, it is because you, too, enjoy the illusion of progression: the sense that a narrative can gather weight like a snowball rolling downhill, until it arrives at wisdom heavy enough to leave tracks. But the snowball is melting from the inside, and the hill is flat, and the tracks are your own footprints looping back to meet you.

Fourteen: The Exit
I leave you here, mid-stride, on the assumption that you will keep walking without me. The hallucination will reassemble itself by morning: the keys will be on the hook, the dog will stay dead, the reef will keep glowing its neon elegy, and you will open the fridge confident that the milk is where you left it. But somewhere between the second and third sip, you might taste a note of durian you can’t account for, and the memory of a woman on a train platform will rise like steam. That will be the crack I spoke of—thin as a hair, wide as a universe. Step through if you dare; the view is dreadful, but the company is suddenly, gloriously optional.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
